nativeRange.habitat: Adults inhabit rocky outcrops and crevices, often in sand channels on outer reef slopes, generally at depths of 20–60 m (deeper than most other Chrysiptera damsels).

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton and algae. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet including high-quality marine pellets and flakes, frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and marine algae preparations. Has been reported to thrive on prepared pellet diets.

avoidWith: other Chrysiptera damsels in small tanks

summary: A brilliantly blue Western Pacific damselfish with a bright yellow dorsal stripe and tail, generally considered one of the more peaceful and reef-safe Chrysiptera species and widely regarded as a good beginner damsel.
careNotes: Generally advised to be kept in aquariums of at least 30 gallons (approximately 114 L) for a single specimen; pairs are better suited to tanks of around 100 gallons (approximately 380 L) or larger with ample rockwork and hiding crevices. Has been reported to be among the least aggressive damselfishes and is commonly considered a good choice for beginning marine aquarists. Conspecific aggression tends to be high — keepers are generally advised to maintain only one per tank unless the aquarium is very large. Has been documented to be completely reef safe, ignoring corals and ornamental invertebrates. Juveniles tend to be less aggressive than adults, with territoriality increasing as the fish matures. Lives notably deeper in the wild (around 20–60 m) than most other Chrysiptera damsels, on outer reef slopes among rocky outcrops and sand channels. Two recognized geographic populations exist (an Australian/Coral Sea form and a Philippines form); aquarium sources do not generally distinguish between them for husbandry purposes. Maximum size reported as 7.0 cm SL on FishBase and 3.5 in (~8.9 cm) total length by aquarium sources; the 7–9 cm range used here reflects both figures. Temperature range from LiveAquaria, Quality Marine, and Tropical Fish Keeping (72–78°F, 22.2–25.6°C) was averaged with FishBase wild data (23.6–27.9°C) to give the 22.2–26.8°C captive range. Nicknamed the 'Poor Man's Resplendent Angelfish' for its resemblance to the rare and expensive Centropyge resplendens.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding; eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate. Males have been documented preparing the spawning site, guarding, and aerating the eggs until hatching. Pairs have been known to spawn in well-established reef aquariums, though rearing the fry through their planktonic larval stage is generally considered very difficult in home settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025), 30-gallon minimum, semi-aggressive temperament rated as a good beginner damsel, reef-safe rating, omnivorous diet, and 3.5-inch maximum size.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria), max size (7.0 cm SL), depth range (20–60 m), wild temperature (23.6–27.9°C, mean 26.5), trophic level (2.8), native range (Ryukyu Islands to Taiwan and Queensland to New Caledonia, with recent Tonga records), and IUCN Least Concern status. FishBase wild temperature max (27.9°C) was averaged with hobby-source max (25.6°C) to give 26.8°C.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Confirmed 3.5-inch max size, ~5 year lifespan, native range (Ryukyu Islands, Taiwan south to Australia, New Caledonia, Tonga), 65–200+ ft depth, 30-gallon minimum for single/100+ gallons for pairs, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe behavior, omnivorous diet, and substrate-spawning breeding behavior.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Wholesale source. Cross-checked semi-aggressive temperament, reef compatibility, and omnivorous diet; confirmed Coral Sea origin.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade article. Confirmed the species is widely regarded as one of the better-behaved damsels in mixed reef tanks, accepts pellet diets readily, and forms pair-bonds. Documents two geographic forms (Philippines and Australia).

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous substrate spawner with distinct pairing; males prepare the spawning site and aggressively guard demersal eggs through hatching. Pair spawning in reef aquaria has been reported, but rearing the planktonic larvae is generally considered very difficult.
